Attestation timing hinges on availability of the qualified attesting person, a scarce-resource constraint. Late attestation delays release and can breach supply commitments, costing revenue.

rows.source_job: Keep recording pace with the line
rows.stmt: Reduce the time between completion of line activity and completion of its batch-record entry
rows.imp_rat: Delay between line activity and record entry challenges contemporaneity but the entry still occurs; moderately relevant with modest consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Auto-timestamps expose latency but do not close it when workload spikes. Contemporaneous prompting shortens the gap, but manual entry during busy operations still forces after-the-fact catch-up. Timely entry depends on the operator balancing recording against physical tasks, a persistent attention burden. Delayed entries risk data-integrity findings and rework that carry real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of analytical data points duplicated in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Duplicated analytical data points are largely rework/clutter caught in review; relevant to clean compilation but low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Structured single-entry fields limit duplication, though multi-system compilation can still introduce duplicates. Duplicate checks run in the background rather than requiring manual scanning. System detection reduces reliance on the analyst spotting duplicates. Duplicated data points cause confusion and rework when reconciled.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time a completed batch awaits required final testing results before disposition
rows.imp_rat: Time a batch awaits final testing before disposition is a delay/throughput factor; relevant to timeliness with largely recoverable consequences.
rows.sat_rat: Automated result posting reduces missed-result surprises, but does not change the fundamental testing lead time driving the wait. The core loss here is delay, and EBR-LIMS integration can shorten it, but batches routinely still wait on external lab turnaround the system cannot accelerate. Little specialized skill is involved; the wait is procedural and testing-throughput bound rather than expertise-limited. Idle batches awaiting results tie up working capital and warehouse space, a cost the status quo only partly mitigates.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a compilation issue remains unassigned because ownership is unclear
rows.imp_rat: An unassigned compilation issue from unclear ownership is a workflow/coordination gap; relevant to closure but consequences are largely internal delay.
rows.sat_rat: Workflow assignment rules help, but ownership gaps still let some compilation issues sit unassigned. Unassigned issues sit idle until someone notices, adding delay. Assigning ownership needs coordination judgement rather than system logic. Idle unassigned issues delay release, a real cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of applying a correction to the wrong batch, manufacturing step, or field
rows.imp_rat: Applying a correction to the wrong batch/step/field corrupts the wrong record (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked lower.
rows.sat_rat: Field-scoped correction dialogs make wrong-target corrections fairly uncommon. Selecting the field to correct is quick within the record context. In-context correction reduces the operator's chance of picking the wrong target, though care is still needed. Misapplied corrections corrupt the wrong record, causing rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrections lacking the original entry, revised entry, reason, attribution, or sequence
rows.imp_rat: Corrections lacking original/revised/reason/attribution violate audit-trail rules (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Audit-trail-enforced correction fields make incomplete correction metadata uncommon. Structured correction dialogs prompt all required elements quickly. Mandatory reason/attribution fields remove reliance on operators knowing what to capture. Corrections missing elements are integrity findings, largely prevented by enforced fields.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a batch decision relying on an erroneous entry before correction is complete
rows.imp_rat: A batch decision relying on an erroneous pre-correction entry risks a bad disposition (severity), but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Little in the status quo prevents an interim decision on an uncorrected value, so risk persists. Corrections are timestamped, but a decision can still be made before the correction posts. Avoiding reliance on erroneous data depends on reviewer awareness of pending corrections. Decisions on erroneous entries can misdispose batches, a high-cost error.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that completion is attested while assigned tests remain unfinished
rows.imp_rat: Attesting completion while tests remain unfinished falsifies status (severity), but ranking holds it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: EBR completeness gating reliably prevents attesting while tracked tests remain unfinished. Completion checks at attestation are instant versus manual verification. System enforcement reduces reliance on the signer verifying all tests are done. Blocking premature attestation avoids costly incomplete releases.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of exceptional test outcomes lacking required documentation at attestation
rows.imp_rat: Exceptional test outcomes lacking documentation at attestation affect record completeness, but as a lower-ranked defect it stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Completeness checks rarely enforce exceptional-outcome documentation specifically, so gaps remain. Attestation checks flag missing documentation quickly when configured. Recognizing exceptional outcomes needing documentation depends on analyst judgement. Undocumented exceptional outcomes are serious compliance costs.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of test dispositions conflicting with analytical values or acceptance criteria
rows.imp_rat: Dispositions conflicting with analytical values or criteria risk wrong release decisions (severity), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where limit checks are configured conflicts are flagged reliably, but manual dispositions still allow inconsistencies. Automated spec comparison flags conflicts faster than manual checking. System acceptance-criteria checks reduce reliance on analyst judgement. Disposition conflicting with data is a high-cost compliance failure.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a correction is applied to the wrong batch, sample, test, or result entry
rows.imp_rat: A correction applied to the wrong batch/sample/test corrupts the wrong record (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked lower.
rows.sat_rat: Entry-specific correction interfaces reduce but do not eliminate misapplied corrections. Context-targeted corrections avoid time fixing the wrong entry later. Selecting the correct entry to fix still requires analyst attention. Corrections applied to the wrong entry compound errors at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of dependent calculations, assessments, or batch-status fields retaining the erroneous value after correction
rows.imp_rat: Dependent fields retaining erroneous values after correction propagates error (severity), but held at 4.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Linked calculations often recompute, but disconnected assessments and status fields can retain stale values. Automatic recalculation of dependents after correction saves manual re-work time. Cascading updates reduce reliance on analyst re-deriving dependents. Stale dependent values can misdirect disposition at high cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a corrected entry is completed without required review
rows.imp_rat: A corrected entry completed without required review is a control gap of real severity, but held at 4.0 by the batch cap.
rows.sat_rat: Correction-review workflows are a fairly mature control, reliably requiring review where configured. Review workflows route corrections quickly but add a controlled step. Workflow gating reduces reliance on analysts remembering to seek review. Enforced review prevents costly unreviewed corrections.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a documented atypical result is unavailable during batch review
rows.imp_rat: An unavailable atypical result during batch review is relevant to compilation completeness, but under the batch-2 ceiling and as a review-stage gap it is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: If captured in the EBR it is reliably retrievable, but atypical data often lives in separate systems and can be missing at review. Electronic retrieval is fast once documented, but scattered raw data slows access. Locating atypical documentation at review may require system familiarity. Unavailable atypical documentation at review delays release at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of results recorded against the wrong sequence step
rows.imp_rat: Results recorded against the wrong sequence step misrepresent execution order (moderate severity/relevance), held at the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Sequenced execution reduces misplacement, but off-sequence or override actions still cause misrecording. Guided sequencing keeps entry aligned to the current step without delay. Step-driven workflows reduce reliance on analyst tracking of position. Results against the wrong step cause rework and confusion at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of results attributed to samples from the wrong batch
rows.imp_rat: Results attributed to the wrong batch is a serious data-integrity error, but the ranking constraint holds it at the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Context-driven batch linkage is fairly reliable but manual selection leaves residual wrong-batch risk. Batch linkage at entry avoids downstream time reattributing samples. Guided batch selection reduces analyst judgement burden. Wrong-batch attribution is a high-cost failure if it reaches disposition.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of results associated with the wrong sampling location or collection point
rows.imp_rat: Results tied to wrong sampling location misattribute data (moderate severity), but under the batch-2 cap it stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Location data is often entered manually and not tightly validated, so misattribution recurs. Capturing sampling location at collection avoids later reconstruction time. Correctly recording sampling point depends on analyst knowledge of the plan. Wrong location attribution can invalidate results at real cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sample identifiers assigned to different batch samples
rows.imp_rat: Duplicate sample identifiers across batch samples threaten traceability (moderate severity and relevance), held at the batch ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Where the system assigns unique IDs duplicates are rare, but manual assignment can still collide. System-generated unique IDs avoid time resolving duplicate assignments. Automated ID assignment removes analyst effort and error. Duplicate identifiers can cause mixups with costly consequences.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that batch-record content becomes corrupted or inaccessible before review
rows.imp_rat: Record content corrupted or inaccessible before review threatens the record itself (severity), but ranking caps it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Validated, backed-up EBR systems make record corruption or inaccessibility rare — a strong status-quo point. Backups and redundancy mean little time is lost to corruption events. Validated infrastructure removes reliance on staff to safeguard record integrity. Robust storage avoids costly data-loss recovery.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of batch-record fields requiring correction during compilation
rows.imp_rat: Fields requiring correction during compilation is routine rework absorbed in the workflow; relevant to efficiency but low consequence.
rows.sat_rat: Field validation catches many errors at entry, but fields still frequently require correction during compilation. Corrections during compilation add rework time, though inline validation reduces their number. Point-of-entry validation reduces reliance on downstream reviewers to catch field errors. Each correction consumes reviewer effort, a recurring moderate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of production runs with required source records absent from the inspection package
rows.imp_rat: Missing source records from the inspection package hurts inspection-readiness (severity), but it ranks lower here and stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Linkage improves inclusion, but cross-system source records are still sometimes absent from the package. Assembling inspection packages with all source records is time-consuming when records span systems. Knowing the complete source-record set relies on experienced reviewers. Missing source records in inspection risk findings and remediation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a nonconforming batch-record management approach is judged conforming
rows.imp_rat: Judging a nonconforming approach as conforming carries real compliance severity, but per ranking it is capped at 4.0 in this batch.
rows.sat_rat: False acceptance of nonconforming approaches is a well-known chronic failure mode that current subjective assessment poorly guards against. Reaching a defensible conformity judgement takes extended deliberation. Distinguishing true conformance requires senior regulatory judgement that is scarce. A false-positive conformance can be enormously costly in later remediation.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of modified batch-record entries whose prior values cannot be reconstructed
rows.imp_rat: Modified entries whose prior values cannot be reconstructed breaks audit-trail integrity (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Contemporary EBR systems reliably retain prior values through audit trails, so this is well served where such systems are deployed. Modern audit-trail features make reconstructing prior values fast. Standard EBR audit trails reduce reliance on specialist effort to recover values. Built-in audit-trail capability makes this low incremental cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record changes lacking a documented reason
rows.imp_rat: Changes lacking a documented reason violate data-integrity expectations (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Mandatory reason-for-change is a mature EBR control, though free-text reasons can still be vague. Reason-for-change prompts capture rationale in real time with no delay. Enforced reason fields reduce dependence on user discipline. Feature is standard and adds negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of sterile-product exposure while required batch entries are completed
rows.imp_rat: Prolonged sterile-product exposure while entries complete has contamination severity, but it is peripheral to the record job and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Nothing in typical EBR practice shortens exposure during in-isolator recording, leaving this largely unaddressed. Time completing entries prolongs sterile-product exposure, and manual EBR entry adds to that duration. Minimizing exposure depends on operator dexterity managing gloves and interface simultaneously. Extended exposure raises contamination risk and potential scrap cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of interruptions to critical aseptic manipulations caused by recording demands
rows.imp_rat: Interruptions to aseptic manipulations by recording demands carry contamination severity, but as peripheral to record compilation it is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: The status quo forces a trade-off between recording and manipulation, poorly mitigated today. Recording demands interrupt critical aseptic manipulations because entry cannot be deferred safely. Managing the interruption relies on operator judgement about when to pause manipulation. Interruptions to aseptic work raise contamination risk and potential batch loss.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of recording equipment usage when the equipment status is unacceptable for the batch step
rows.imp_rat: Recording equipment usage when status is unacceptable has quality severity, but is a lower-ranked defect held at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Only some EBRs enforce equipment-status checks, so this remains partially controlled. Status checks against equipment (calibration, clean) can be instant where integrated. System status gating reduces reliance on operators verifying equipment acceptability. Using unacceptable-status equipment can invalidate a batch, a high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material-usage records attributed to a batch step where consumption did not occur
rows.imp_rat: Material-usage records at steps where consumption did not occur are false entries (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Step-scoped material entry limits this, but nothing fully prevents mis-stepped consumption records. Correcting a misattributed consumption record is quick but relies on noticing it. Preventing consumption records on non-consuming steps depends on operator attention. False consumption records corrupt reconciliation, causing investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of recorded step-sequence changes without corresponding line activity
rows.imp_rat: Recorded sequence changes without corresponding line activity signal integrity concerns (severity), but ranked lower here.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails expose sequence changes but do not confirm corresponding line activity occurred. Recording a sequence change is quick but verifying it matches the line takes attention. Preventing unwarranted sequence changes depends on operator and reviewer judgement. Phantom sequence changes confuse review and cause rework.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of a corrected value conflicting with contemporaneous manufacturing evidence
rows.imp_rat: A corrected value conflicting with contemporaneous evidence signals inconsistency (severity), kept below ceiling as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Little cross-checks a corrected value against other records, leaving this weakly controlled. Checking a corrected value against other evidence takes reviewer time not aided by the tool. Ensuring consistency with contemporaneous evidence relies on reviewer expertise. A corrected value conflicting with evidence is a serious integrity concern with high cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of valid entries unintentionally altered during correction
rows.imp_rat: Valid entries unintentionally altered during correction damage good data (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Field-scoped edits make unintended alteration of adjacent valid entries uncommon. Correcting one field without touching others is fast in field-level edit modes. Field-level correction reduces reliance on operator care not to disturb valid entries. Altering valid entries during correction causes avoidable rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between an aseptic intervention and completion of its documentation
rows.imp_rat: Delay between aseptic intervention and its documentation risks data-integrity gaps (severity), but timing lag is peripheral to core compilation and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Deferred entry raises the chance of forgotten or inaccurate detail, weakly controlled today. Aseptic priorities force intervention documentation to be deferred until manipulations end, extending the delay. Timing depends on operator judgement to balance sterile work against recording. Delayed intervention documentation risks integrity findings and investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of discrepancy between flagged exception details and the observed line condition
rows.imp_rat: Discrepancy between flagged exception and observed line condition undermines record accuracy (severity moderate), but it is a narrower exception-detail issue kept below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Structured fields help, but free-text condition descriptions still diverge from observation. Flagging in real time is possible, but detail accuracy suffers when done after the fact. Matching flagged detail to the real line condition relies on operator observation and judgement. Discrepant exception details cause misassessment and rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required supporting analytical records unavailable after attestation
rows.imp_rat: Supporting analytical records unavailable after attestation weakens defensibility (severity), but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: EBR-stored records are retrievable, but supporting data in external systems can be unavailable at review. Electronic archival keeps records retrievable quickly after attestation. Centralized storage reduces reliance on analysts locating supporting records. Unavailable supporting records delay release and audits at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of post-attestation documentation changes lacking reviewer visibility
rows.imp_rat: Post-attestation changes lacking reviewer visibility are an integrity concern (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Locked records with audit trails reliably surface post-attestation changes to reviewers. Post-attestation change logging is automatic, requiring no analyst time. System versioning removes reliance on manual change notification. Visible post-attestation changes prevent costly hidden alterations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of corrected entries whose original value or correction history is obscured
rows.imp_rat: Obscured original values or correction history breaks audit reconstruction (severity), but capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: EBR audit trails reliably preserve original values and correction history, a core strength. Original values and history are retained automatically with no added time. Audit-trail retention removes reliance on analyst preserving history. Preserved correction history avoids costly integrity questions.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a testing entry is changed by an unauthorized executor
rows.imp_rat: A testing entry changed by an unauthorized executor is an access-control breach (severity), but capped at 4.0 as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Role-based access control is a mature, strong EBR feature that reliably blocks unauthorized editors. Role-based access enforcement is instantaneous with no analyst effort. Permission controls remove reliance on manual gatekeeping. Preventing unauthorized edits avoids costly integrity investigations.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of corrected entries lacking executor or date attribution
rows.imp_rat: Corrected entries lacking executor or date attribution violate basic audit rules (severity), but held at 4.0 by ranking.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails reliably attach executor and date to corrections, a mature control. Executor and date are captured automatically at correction with no delay. System attribution removes any skill burden for annotating corrections. Reliable correction attribution avoids costly documentation gaps.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition changes lacking performer attribution
rows.imp_rat: Condition changes lacking performer attribution weaken audit trail (severity), but this item is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: User-context and audit trails reliably attribute condition changes to a performer. Login-based attribution of condition changes is instantaneous. System attribution removes skill burden for naming the performer. Reliable performer attribution avoids costly documentation queries.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of condition entries misstating when the observation occurred
rows.imp_rat: Condition entries misstating observation timing distort the record (severity), kept below the ceiling as ranked.
rows.sat_rat: Entry timestamps are reliable but may reflect recording time, not observation time, allowing mismatch. Automatic timestamping avoids manual time-of-observation entry. System capture reduces reliance on analyst recording when observations occurred. Wrong observation timing can mislead reviews at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of atypical-result records in which interpretation is indistinguishable from the original observation
rows.imp_rat: Interpretation indistinguishable from original observation blurs data integrity (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Free-text atypical documentation often mixes observation and interpretation, a persistent weakness. Separating observation from interpretation adds documentation time. Distinguishing raw observation from interpretation is a judgement-heavy skill task. Blended records complicate investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of atypical-result records missing context needed to interpret the observation
rows.imp_rat: Atypical-result records missing interpretive context harm reviewability (severity), but capped at 4.0 as a lower-ranked defect.
rows.sat_rat: Context capture for atypical results is unstructured and frequently incomplete. Adding interpretive context in the moment takes analyst time. Knowing what context to capture depends heavily on analyst expertise. Missing context forces investigation rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of atypical-result entries with metadata misrepresenting the event sequence
rows.imp_rat: Metadata misrepresenting event sequence undermines integrity (severity), but the ranking holds it at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: System timestamps are reliable, but manual atypical entries can still misstate the event sequence. Auto-timestamped metadata avoids manual sequence documentation time. System metadata reduces reliance on analyst recording event order. Misrepresented event sequence undermines investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of tests initiated before their prerequisites are complete
rows.imp_rat: Tests initiated before prerequisites complete threaten data validity (severity), but capped at 4.0 as a lower-ranked item.
rows.sat_rat: Where prerequisite logic is configured it works, but many EBRs enforce it only partially. Prerequisite gating prevents time wasted on prematurely started tests. Automated prerequisite checks reduce reliance on analyst vigilance. Tests started before prerequisites can be wasted effort at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that the wrong conditional path is followed after a result
rows.imp_rat: Following the wrong conditional path after a result can propagate errors (severity), but ranks lower and stays at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configured decision logic helps but complex or manual branches still allow wrong-path selection. Conditional branching in workflows routes automatically, saving decision time. Automated branching reduces reliance on analyst interpreting result-driven paths. A wrong conditional path can invalidate downstream work at cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that testing resumes at the wrong step after an interruption
rows.imp_rat: Resuming testing at the wrong step after interruption risks invalid results (severity), but held at 4.0 by the ranking constraint.
rows.sat_rat: Persistent workflow state helps resume correctly, but interruptions still occasionally lead to wrong-step resumption. State-saving lets execution resume without re-doing steps, saving time. System-tracked position reduces reliance on analyst memory after interruptions. Resuming at the wrong step causes rework at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of analyses assigned a sequence conflicting with the approved specification
rows.imp_rat: Analyses sequenced against the approved specification risk validity (severity), but this is a lower-ranked defect capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Configured workflows enforce approved order fairly reliably where built in, but ad hoc analyses can conflict. Specification-driven sequencing avoids time reordering analyses. Enforced order reduces reliance on analyst knowing the approved sequence. Sequence conflicts with specification can invalidate results at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested samples with missing or unreadable identity information
rows.imp_rat: Missing or unreadable sample identity information harms traceability (severity moderate), but as an ambiguity item it stays under the 4.0 ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Electronic capture makes unreadable identity rare compared to paper, though missing entries still occur. Scanned identifiers are captured quickly and legibly, saving reconciliation time. Barcode capture removes the skill burden of transcribing identity. Unreadable identity forces re-sampling or investigation at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of tested samples with conflicting identity information across records
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting identity information across records for tested samples affects traceability (severity), yet remains a lower-ranked instability item under 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: When integrated with LIMS/sample management identity is consistent, but cross-record conflicts persist where systems disconnect. Single sample-identity source reduces time reconciling conflicts. System-carried identity lowers reliance on analyst re-keying. Conflicting sample identities cause investigations at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of tested aliquots lacking traceability to their originating batch sample
rows.imp_rat: Aliquots lacking traceability to originating batch sample weakens data lineage (severity), kept below ceiling as a lower-ranked item.
rows.sat_rat: Aliquot-level traceability is often weakly supported, so links back to the originating sample can be lost. Aliquot traceability chains take time to maintain across steps. Maintaining aliquot-to-sample links requires analyst diligence. Loss of aliquot traceability can force re-testing at cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of sample-result associations changed without attribution
rows.imp_rat: Sample-result associations changed without attribution weaken audit integrity (severity), held at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Audit trails reliably attribute sample-result association changes when they occur in-system. Audit trails capture association changes automatically without extra time. System logging removes reliance on analyst documenting changes. Attributed association changes avoid costly disputes.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that a verified value is changed without renewed verification
rows.imp_rat: A verified value changed without renewed verification undermines control (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Some systems re-open verification on change, but this control is inconsistently implemented. Re-verification triggers add a controlled step but limit downstream time. Recognizing that a verified value changed relies partly on analyst awareness. Changing verified values without re-check risks costly errors.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of required sterile fill-finish entries posted after scheduled checkpoints in each run
rows.imp_rat: Sterile fill-finish entries posted after checkpoints raise integrity concern (severity), but this delay item is capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Checkpoint prompts help, but entries still frequently post after their scheduled points. Fill-finish entries posted after checkpoints represent recurring timeliness slippage. Timely posting depends on operator discipline under production pressure. Late entries risk sterile-batch rework, a high-value cost exposure.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration between generation of required production data and its inclusion in the batch record
rows.imp_rat: Delay including generated production data into the batch record touches completeness (relevance), but as a timing item severity is low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Where interfaced, data lands promptly; where manual, it lags, giving mixed but improving performance. System integration shortens the lag between data generation and inclusion, though manual transfers still add latency. Automated data flow reduces reliance on staff to transcribe or import data. Latency ties up compilation resources but is a modest cost where integration exists.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time from production-run completion until the compiled batch record is inspection-ready
rows.imp_rat: Time to inspection-ready compiled record touches the compilation job (relevance), but as a delay its severity is low and capped.
rows.sat_rat: EBR speeds parts of the flow, but end-to-end inspection-readiness time is still driven by manual bottlenecks. Time from run completion to inspection-ready remains long due to sequential review and exception closure. Reaching inspection-ready depends on scarce reviewer and QA capacity. Long compilation cycles delay release and tie up working capital.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of applicable regulatory expectations omitted from the assessment scope
rows.imp_rat: Regulatory expectations omitted from assessment scope has compliance severity, but is a lower-ranked scoping item capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Well-known regulations are usually captured, though niche or updated expectations are sometimes missed. Assembling applicable regulatory expectations from known sources is reasonably quick with existing libraries. Requires regulatory literacy but the core expectation set is well documented. Regulatory reference material is largely free and reusable.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of regulated batch-record data and metadata lifecycle obligations omitted from validation requirements
rows.imp_rat: Omitting lifecycle obligations from validation requirements has compliance severity, but it is a validation-scoping concern ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Metadata lifecycle obligations are commonly under-specified, so omissions from validation requirements are a persistent gap. Enumerating every data and metadata lifecycle obligation is a lengthy manual exercise. Requires specialized understanding of metadata and record-lifecycle regulatory expectations that is scarce. Missed obligations trigger costly revalidation, though the assessment itself is inexpensive.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of regulated batch-data migration and reconciliation requirements omitted from validation scope
rows.imp_rat: Omitting migration/reconciliation requirements from validation scope has compliance severity, but is a lower-ranked scoping item capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Migration and reconciliation requirements are frequently omitted from scope, a recurring and poorly mitigated weakness. Defining migration and reconciliation validation scope is a slow, detail-heavy task. Data-migration validation is a niche specialty many teams lack in depth. Missed migration requirements cause expensive data-integrity failures post-go-live.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of relevant batch-record action types omitted from traceability requirements
rows.imp_rat: Action types omitted from traceability requirements creates audit gaps (severity), but capped at 4.0 as a lower-ranked item.
rows.sat_rat: Action-type coverage is usually mostly complete but edge-case actions are periodically omitted. Listing all batch-record action types for traceability is a moderate effort. Requires process knowledge of every action a batch record undergoes. Omitted action types cause rework but the enumeration itself is cheap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record actions lacking unambiguous originator attribution
rows.imp_rat: Actions lacking unambiguous originator attribution weaken accountability (severity), but held below the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Unique-login attribution is a well-established, highly reliable control in compliant EBR systems. Automatic user attribution requires no extra time. Identity binding removes the need for manual attribution judgement. Attribution is inherent in authenticated systems at little added cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record actions lacking consistent chronological context
rows.imp_rat: Actions lacking consistent chronological context undermines timeline integrity (severity), capped at 4.0.
rows.sat_rat: Chronological context via synchronized timestamps is a robust, standard capability, with only clock-sync edge cases remaining. System-generated timestamps are instantaneous. Automated timestamping removes manual effort entirely. Timestamping is built in with negligible cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of batch-record actions from connected record sources lacking end-to-end lineage
rows.imp_rat: Connected-source actions lacking end-to-end lineage weakens traceability (severity), but ranked lower and capped.
rows.sat_rat: Within a single system lineage is good, but end-to-end across connected sources remains inconsistently achieved. Tracing end-to-end lineage across connected sources still requires manual stitching. Cross-system lineage demands integration expertise to establish and verify. Lineage gaps drive costly investigations across systems.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of valid process-data entries rejected during recording
rows.imp_rat: Valid process-data entries rejected during recording is rework of modest severity; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Overly strict or misconfigured validation intermittently rejects valid data, a recurring nuisance. Valid entries wrongly rejected force re-entry, wasting time. Understanding and overriding false rejections depends on operator familiarity with validation rules. False rejections cause delay and workarounds but rarely product cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of losing an in-progress entry after an access interruption
rows.imp_rat: Losing an in-progress entry after access interruption causes re-entry rework; modest stakes hold it low.
rows.sat_rat: Server-side session persistence makes losing an in-progress entry uncommon in mature EBRs. Auto-save and session recovery restore work quickly after interruptions in modern systems. Recovery features remove reliance on operators remembering to save. Lost in-progress entries force re-entry, a cost auto-save largely avoids.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of equipment-usage entries linked to the wrong batch step
rows.imp_rat: Equipment-usage entries linked to wrong batch step are traceability errors of modest scope; kept low under the cap.
rows.sat_rat: Step-scoped equipment entry makes wrong-step linkage fairly uncommon. Preset step context means correct equipment linkage costs no extra time. Contextual binding reduces operator need to select the correct step for equipment. Wrong-step equipment linkage causes traceability review cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of ambiguity in matching line equipment to its batch-record identifier
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity matching line equipment to its batch-record identifier is a traceability nuisance of modest impact; capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Scanning resolves ambiguity where labels exist, but unlabeled or shared equipment leaves gaps. Matching physical equipment to its ID can slow entry when labels are unclear on the floor. Correct matching depends on operator familiarity with equipment tagging conventions. Ambiguous matching causes wrong IDs and traceability rework.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time between material consumption and entry of its usage record

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of projected batch-review and release savings unsupported by site evidence
rows.imp_rat: Unsupported savings projections are a financial-evidence gap peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Projected review and release savings are frequently asserted without site evidence, an unreliable basis. Gathering site evidence to support projected savings is a lengthy validation exercise. Validating savings claims against operational data needs analytical and process expertise beyond procurement. Unsupported savings projections overstate value and misallocate funds.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of acquisition, validation, integration, training, maintenance, and change-control cost omitted from the investment case
rows.imp_rat: Cost categories omitted from the investment case is a financial-completeness error peripheral to the record-compilation job, with budget-correction stakes, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Coverage of these categories varies widely between cases with no enforced checklist, making omissions predictable. Gathering acquisition, validation, integration, training, maintenance and change-control estimates from many functions is time-consuming and often delayed. Estimating validation and change-control cost for regulated EBR systems requires specialist GxP knowledge rarely resident in procurement. These downstream categories are the most commonly omitted, so the investment case routinely understates true cost.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of financial benefit counted more than once across labor savings, release acceleration, and avoided deviations
rows.imp_rat: Double-counting benefits across savings categories is a financial-case artifact, not central to record compilation (low relevance); consequences are recoverable case adjustments (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Without disciplined benefit attribution, double counting recurs across benefit categories. Detecting double-counted benefits requires careful cross-checking that adds review time. Spotting overlap among labor, release and deviation savings requires analytical rigor often missing. Double-counted benefits inflate the case and lead to overfunding.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of material financial assumptions that conflict across finance, quality, manufacturing, testing, and procurement stakeholders
rows.imp_rat: Conflicting financial assumptions across stakeholders is a case-alignment concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Assumption conflicts across stakeholders routinely persist unresolved into the decision. Reconciling assumptions across finance, quality, manufacturing, testing and procurement requires lengthy cross-functional alignment. Surfacing conflicting assumptions across many stakeholders needs facilitation and domain fluency that few individuals hold. Conflicting financial assumptions produce a case that cannot be trusted for funding.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between modeled cash-flow timing and the site validation, rollout, adoption, and production-disruption schedule
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between modeled cash-flow timing and the schedule is a financial-modeling error tangential to the CFJ, with forecast consequences, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Cash-flow timing assumptions rarely track actual adoption and disruption schedules, making them consistently off. Aligning modeled cash-flow timing with the real validation and rollout schedule is rarely done accurately, so timing errors persist. Synchronizing financial timing to a complex deployment schedule requires joint finance and program skill that is scarce. Timing mismatches distort NPV and cash planning, misstating the financial case.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of mismatch between the scope costed in the financial case and the scope required for site manufacturing and testing records
rows.imp_rat: Mismatch between costed and required scope is a financial-scoping error tangential to compiling records, with budget-alignment consequences, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Without a rigorous scope-to-requirement traceability practice, mismatches slip through inconsistently. Reconciling costed scope against the site's actual manufacturing and testing record requirements takes iterative cross-functional review. Judging whether costed scope matches all site record requirements needs deep manufacturing and QA process knowledge beyond procurement's reach. Scope mismatches surface as unbudgeted change orders and rework spend after authorization.

rows.stmt: Minimize the degree of variation in expected return under plausible changes in batch volume, product mix, labor cost, and rollout schedule
rows.imp_rat: Variation in expected return under changing assumptions is a financial-sensitivity error tangential to the CFJ, with forecast consequences, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Returns are typically presented as single points with no robustness testing, so variation is unaddressed. Running scenarios across volume, mix, labor and schedule variations adds analysis time. Sensitivity and scenario modeling demands analytical expertise usually absent from base cases. Unmodeled variation means the realized return can fall well short of the funded expectation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of throughput-critical manufacturing or testing workflows omitted from the authorized scope
rows.imp_rat: Throughput-critical workflows omitted from scope affects coverage but the ranking caps this below the prior batch (low relevance/severity enforced).
rows.sat_rat: Scope definition often misses some critical workflows without a rigorous coverage check. Confirming that all throughput-critical workflows are in scope requires time-consuming process walkthroughs. Knowing which workflows are throughput-critical demands deep manufacturing and testing process knowledge outside procurement. Omitted critical workflows drive unbudgeted scope additions later.

rows.stmt: Minimize the duration of site production disruption during the transition to electronic record management
rows.imp_rat: Production disruption during transition is a change-management concern peripheral to compiling records (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Transition disruption is a common and somewhat predictable feature of EBR go-live. Transition to electronic records commonly disrupts production for a meaningful period. Minimizing transition disruption requires change-management and validation expertise that is scarce. Production disruption during transition carries direct cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the number of manual record transfers and reconciliations remaining between manufacturing and testing activities
rows.imp_rat: Manual transfers/reconciliations remaining is an efficiency concern peripheral to compiling records at this rank (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Manual record transfers remain a common source of error and inconsistency. Manual transfers and reconciliations between manufacturing and testing add handoff delay. Eliminating manual reconciliation depends on integration design expertise outside procurement. Manual transfers consume labor and introduce rework cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of batch execution or review interruptions caused by unavailable electronic batch records
rows.imp_rat: Execution/review interruptions from unavailable records tie to the job but the ranking caps this below the prior batch (low relevance/severity enforced).
rows.sat_rat: Interruptions from unavailable records are an intermittent but real operational reliability issue. Record unavailability interrupts execution and review, directly costing time during batch processing. Preventing these interruptions is a systems-reliability matter handled by IT rather than the executor. Interruptions cause throughput loss with associated cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the percentage of batches placed on hold because record data is missing, inconsistent, or untraceable
rows.imp_rat: Batches held for missing/untraceable data ties directly to the job but the ranking caps this below the prior batch (low relevance/severity enforced).
rows.sat_rat: Missing or inconsistent record data causing holds is a persistent operational reliability concern. Batches on hold for missing or untraceable data wait through investigation, extending cycle time. Ensuring data completeness and traceability requires process and system design expertise outside procurement. Holds tie up inventory and drive investigation cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of batch-record review from completed execution to disposition readiness
rows.imp_rat: Review duration to disposition readiness is a cycle-time concern peripheral to the record job at this rank (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Review duration is variable and often long, an outcome the executor funds but does not directly control. Batch-record review from execution to disposition readiness is a well-known bottleneck consuming significant time. Review speed depends on reviewer expertise and system usability that vary widely. Extended review ties up working capital and delays release revenue.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of record exceptions requiring investigation or correction before batch disposition
rows.imp_rat: Record exceptions requiring investigation before disposition ties to the job but the ranking caps this below the prior batch (low relevance/severity enforced).
rows.sat_rat: Record exceptions before disposition are a common operational reliability issue. Record exceptions requiring investigation before disposition add delay each time they occur. Reducing exceptions depends on system and process design expertise outside the executor's control. Investigation and correction of exceptions consumes labor cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time manufacturing and testing records wait for required review or approval during batch disposition
rows.imp_rat: Records waiting for review/approval is a cycle-time concern peripheral to the record job at this rank (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Approval wait times are variable and a persistent disposition bottleneck. Records waiting for review or approval during disposition is a direct and frequent source of delay. Reducing approval waits depends on workflow design and reviewer availability beyond the executor's control. Waiting records delay release and tie up inventory value.

rows.stmt: Minimize the amount of EBR lifecycle cost omitted from the acquisition financial case
rows.imp_rat: Lifecycle cost omitted from the financial case is a costing error tangential to the record-compilation CFJ, with budget-correction consequences, capped at the ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: There is no consistent practice guaranteeing all lifecycle cost categories are captured, so omissions recur case to case. Assembling a full lifecycle cost view takes considerable effort because cost data is scattered across vendor quotes, IT, validation, and operations. Building a complete total-cost-of-ownership model for a complex EBR system demands scarce cost-engineering and domain judgement that most procurement teams lack. Lifecycle costs like validation, change control, and maintenance are chronically underestimated or omitted from the initial case, driving material overspend later.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount by which EBR implementation, integration, and validation spending exceeds the authorized financial case
rows.imp_rat: Implementation spending exceeding the authorized case is a budget-overrun error peripheral to compiling records, with financial consequences that are recoverable, capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Actuals overrunning the case is a recurring pattern for complex regulated system deployments. Overruns typically surface late during implementation rather than in real time, so corrective response is delayed. Controlling implementation, integration and validation spend against the case requires program cost-control discipline not always present. EBR implementation and validation spending very commonly overruns the authorized case due to unforeseen configuration and validation effort.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood of EBR data-integrity failures causing batch holds, investigations, or remediation expense
rows.imp_rat: Data-integrity failures causing batch holds ties to the record job but the ranking caps it below the prior batch (low relevance/severity enforced).
rows.sat_rat: This is an operational outcome the executor funds but does not control, and residual data-integrity risk remains real. Data-integrity failures trigger batch holds and investigations that consume substantial time when they occur. Preventing and diagnosing data-integrity failures requires specialist QA and validation expertise beyond procurement. Such failures drive costly batch holds, investigations and remediation.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of production loss and recovery expense caused by EBR unavailability
rows.imp_rat: Production loss from EBR unavailability affects operations but the ranking caps it below the prior batch (low relevance/severity enforced).
rows.sat_rat: Availability is addressed by SLAs and infrastructure but downtime events still occur. System unavailability causes production loss whose recovery takes time when it happens. Ensuring availability through redundancy and support requires IT and validation skill outside the executor's role. Unavailability drives direct production loss and recovery expense.

rows.stmt: Reduce the duration of overlapping legacy-record and EBR operating costs caused by deployment delays
rows.imp_rat: Overlapping legacy and EBR costs from delays is a transition-cost concern peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Deployment delays that extend overlap are a recurring pattern in these programs. Deployment delays extend the period of parallel operating costs, prolonging the burden. Controlling deployment timelines requires program-management skill that is variably available. Running legacy and EBR systems in parallel duplicates operating cost during overlap.

rows.stmt: Minimize the percentage of forecast labor, material, and compliance savings not realized over the EBR useful life
rows.imp_rat: Forecast savings not realized over the useful life is a benefit-realization error peripheral to compiling records, with financial consequences, capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Benefits realization is seldom tracked to closure, so unrealized savings are the norm rather than the exception. Realization of forecast savings plays out over the whole useful life, so shortfalls take years to confirm. Tracking and attributing realized savings requires benefits-realization discipline rarely staffed. A large share of forecast labor, material and compliance savings is commonly never realized.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of recurring EBR charges above the escalation assumptions in the financial case
rows.imp_rat: Recurring charges above escalation assumptions is a cost-forecast error peripheral to compiling records, with budget consequences, capped low.
rows.sat_rat: Escalation assumptions are frequently too optimistic, so actuals reliably drift above them. Recurring charge overruns emerge over years, so detection is slow relative to when assumptions were set. Forecasting recurring charge escalation requires modeling skill often applied only superficially. Recurring charges routinely exceed optimistic escalation assumptions, eroding the case.

rows.stmt: Reduce the frequency of post-authorization requirement changes that trigger EBR configuration and validation rework
rows.imp_rat: Post-authorization requirement changes triggering rework is a change-management concern indirect to compiling records (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Post-authorization requirement changes are a recurring source of rework in EBR projects. Post-authorization requirement changes trigger rework cycles that consume significant time. Freezing requirements adequately upfront requires elicitation skill that is often insufficient. Configuration and validation rework from requirement changes is a major cost driver.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that supplier support ends before the assumed EBR useful life
rows.imp_rat: Supplier support ending before useful life is a vendor-continuity risk, indirect to record compilation (low relevance); ranking caps severity low.
rows.sat_rat: Support-continuity risk can be partly mitigated by contract terms but long-horizon vendor viability remains genuinely uncertain. Assessing vendor longevity and support commitment is part of standard vendor vetting timelines. Evaluating supplier viability over a useful life requires market and financial-health judgement that procurement typically has. Premature support end forces costly early replacement or migration.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in supplier pricing, escalation, and usage terms for the EBR system
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in supplier pricing terms is a contracting-clarity error peripheral to compiling records, with commercial consequences, held at ceiling.
rows.sat_rat: Standard contracting practice reduces ambiguity but usage-based and escalation terms for software often remain unclear. Clarifying pricing, escalation and usage terms takes negotiation rounds that add time. Interpreting complex escalation and usage clauses is core procurement skill that is generally available. Ambiguous escalation and usage terms create unpredictable future charges.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that patient-safety, data-integrity, or compliance exposure is understated when evaluating competing site initiatives
rows.imp_rat: Understating patient-safety/compliance exposure when comparing initiatives is an evaluation error touching serious stakes, but the ranking mandates the ceiling, so both proxies stay capped despite higher intrinsic severity.
rows.sat_rat: There is no consistent method to ensure safety and compliance exposure is fully reflected, so it is often understated. Weighing safety and compliance exposure across competing initiatives lengthens the evaluation cycle. Properly valuing patient-safety and data-integrity exposure requires cross-domain risk judgement that is scarce. Understated exposure misdirects funding toward lower-value initiatives.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of ambiguity in how patient safety, compliance, product quality, throughput, and financial value are weighted across competing site initiatives
rows.imp_rat: Ambiguity in weighting value criteria across initiatives is a governance concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Weighting is usually implicit and inconsistent, producing unstable prioritization. Reaching agreement on how to weight competing value dimensions consumes prolonged debate. Establishing a defensible weighting across safety, compliance, quality, throughput and finance demands scarce decision-analysis expertise. Ambiguous weighting leads to misallocated funding across initiatives.

rows.stmt: Reduce the degree of inconsistency among cost, benefit, risk, and delivery assumptions in competing initiative business cases
rows.imp_rat: Inconsistent assumptions across business cases is a comparison-quality concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Business cases are typically built independently with no harmonized assumptions, so inconsistency is common. Reconciling assumptions across competing business cases is a slow cross-comparison effort. Detecting inconsistent cost, benefit and risk assumptions across cases requires analytical scrutiny that is scarce. Inconsistent assumptions lead to unfair comparisons and misdirected funds.

rows.stmt: Reduce the amount of benefit counted more than once across overlapping EBR and site initiatives
rows.imp_rat: Benefit double-counting across initiatives is a portfolio-accounting concern peripheral to record compilation (low relevance) with absorbable impact (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Portfolio benefit overlap is seldom systematically reconciled, so it recurs. Cross-checking benefits against other site initiatives is a slow, coordination-heavy task. Recognizing overlapping benefits across a portfolio needs portfolio-level insight rarely held by one executor. Benefits claimed by multiple initiatives lead to portfolio-level overfunding.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that dependencies between the EBR initiative and other site initiatives are omitted from funding decisions
rows.imp_rat: Dependencies omitted from funding decisions is a planning-completeness concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Dependencies between initiatives are frequently omitted from funding decisions. Mapping cross-initiative dependencies is a slow coordination task often skipped under time pressure. Identifying dependencies across the initiative portfolio requires portfolio insight that is scarce. Missed dependencies cause costly rework and stranded investment.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that implementation and validation resources are committed to conflicting initiative schedules
rows.imp_rat: Resources committed to conflicting schedules is a resource-planning concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Implementation and validation resource conflicts across initiatives are frequently discovered only after commitment. Checking for resource schedule conflicts across initiatives takes coordination time. Detecting conflicting resource commitments requires portfolio resource-planning skill that is scarce. Resource conflicts cause delays and rework that inflate cost.

rows.stmt: Reduce the time required to authorize EBR funding after competing initiative cases are decision-ready
rows.imp_rat: Time to authorize funding after cases are ready is a governance-timing concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Authorization timelines after decision-readiness are somewhat variable but generally manageable. Even after cases are decision-ready, final authorization steps still take longer than desired. Driving a decision-ready case to signature requires governance navigation skill that is generally available. Authorization delay defers benefits and prolongs carrying costs.

rows.stmt: Minimize the likelihood that regulatory, production, or financial changes invalidate the initiative ranking before funding authorization
rows.imp_rat: Changes invalidating initiative ranking before funding is a portfolio-timing concern indirect to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).
rows.sat_rat: Rankings are rarely re-validated against late-breaking changes, so they can be stale at authorization. Re-checking initiative ranking against changing conditions before funding adds evaluation time. Judging whether regulatory or production changes invalidate rankings needs cross-functional foresight that is scarce. Funding a stale ranking wastes capital on a no-longer-optimal initiative.

rows.imp_rat: Visible uncertainty when cost assumptions challenged is a composure concern peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).

rows.imp_rat: Losing composure over supplier data-integrity challenges is a personal-emotional concern peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with mild consequences (low severity).

rows.imp_rat: Doubt that releasing funds preserves other budgets is a portfolio concern peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with recoverable consequences (low severity).

rows.imp_rat: Doubt about the release decision under conflicting signals is a decision-confidence concern peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).

rows.imp_rat: Feeling exposed without a defensible funding answer is a preparedness concern peripheral to the record job (low relevance) with contained impact (low severity).
